description:
You are working as a web-developer who has just received a raw Apache
    access log that lives at

        /home/user/project/access.log

    Your goal is to get a quick overview of how the web-server responded to
    recent requests.  Using nothing more than the standard GNU core-utils
    provided in a typical Linux shell (for example: cut, awk, sort, uniq,
    head/tail, cat, etc.), create a small frequency table that shows how many
    times each HTTP status code (e.g. 200, 404, 302 …) appears in that log.

    Requirements for the result:

    1. Read exclusively from /home/user/project/access.log.
    2. Produce a new text file named

           /home/user/project/output/status_frequency.txt
       
       (create the output/ directory if it does not exist).
    3. The file must contain ONE line per distinct status code, formatted
       exactly like this:

           <status_code><single_space><count><newline>

       • <status_code> is the three-digit HTTP status code found in the log  
         (e.g. 200).  
       • <count> is the total number of times that code appears.  
       • Lines must be sorted in descending numerical order by <count>.  
       • No extra leading/trailing spaces.  
       • A final newline must exist after the last line.
    4. Nothing else should be written to that file, and no other files should
       be created or modified.
    5. Accomplish the task with a maximum of four individual shell commands
       (pipelines count as one command).

    When you are done, you can cat /home/user/project/output/status_frequency.txt
    to show the result to the user.
name: task_000000_cbcba77c
